subject: [PATCH resend v6 03/30] mm: thread user_addr through page allocator for cache-friendly zeroing

reviews.author: Michael Tsirkin
reviews.summary: Reviewer questioned the use of user_addr in this patch, suggesting it may not be necessary since faults are rare and proposing an alternative constant FAULT_ADDR_NONE for clarity.
reviews.raw_body:
this is used on a fault. I don't think there are any faults then?
But maybe FAULT_ADDR_NONE would be clearer.

reviews.author: Gregory Price (author)
reviews.summary:
Author acknowledged that the concern about -1 being a valid user address across all architectures is still open, and is seeking clarification on this point.

Author acknowledged the reviewer's suggestion to prefer stack style changelog formatting and agreed that spelling out the allocation process is a good approach.
reviews.raw_body:
Meh, naming here is less relevant than the sentinel correctness.

My only concern is really whether -1 could end up being a valid address
in some horrid future timeline and this all going belly up.

Is why I asked about whether this is correct on all architectures.

---

Yeah simply spelling it out, not asking for a change.  Probably no
better way to go about it.

~Gregory

reviews.author: Michael Tsirkin
reviews.summary: Reviewer questioned whether -1 is a valid user address across all architectures, noting that the kernel enforces this by not creating mappings whose end wraps past -1.
reviews.raw_body:
I think the answer is yes: on all architectures Linux supports, the last
page of the address space is never a valid user mapping. The kernel
enforces this -- mmap will not create a mapping whose end wraps past -1.

  if (addr > TASK_SIZE - len)
      return -ENOMEM;


So the maximum vm_end = addr + len <= TASK_SIZE.
On every architecture TASK_SIZE <= (unsigned long)-1, so vm_end can
never wrap around to 0 and address -1 can never be within any
VMA.

reviews.author: Gregory Price (author)
reviews.summary: Author acknowledged that the preference for stack style documentation should be noted in the changelog or code comments, and agreed to add it.
reviews.raw_body:
That is worth documenting at least in the changelog if not the comment
with the define there's a clue if anything ever changes.

~Gregory

2026-05-11.patch_summary: This patch is about improving the handling of user addresses in memory allocation, specifically to make cache-friendly zeroing more efficient. The current approach involves passing USER_ADDR_NONE through various functions, but this can be simplified by introducing a separate function for user-land bound allocations. This change aims to reduce code churn and potential subtle corruption issues related to cache flushing.
